author: @Excalibur80
text: What happens when this get's shot or beaten with a hammer? Does it fracture like ceramics, or does it bend and absorb the impact like plastic?

replies.author: @michaellovelace3844
replies.text: This has been answered in previous comments, but I'll summarize here. The first sample made with .5% graphene was shot with a .22 and a .45, both rounds from carbines and stopped both with a little deformation. The second was made with 2% graphene and stopped a 7.62x39 round with a little deformation.

replies.author: @normalchannel8493
replies.text: @michaellovelace3844 thanks!

replies.author: @michaellovelace3844
replies.text: @normalchannel8493  In a discussion in the comments, it turns out that you start with a fairly ductile matrix material, the addition of functionalized graphene reduces the ductility but greatly increases the strength. So it's a trade-off, but one which works pretty well.

replies.author: @normalchannel8493
replies.text: @michaellovelace3844 What happens when i put more than the 5% of graphene in the plastic, it gets weaker?

replies.author: @michaellovelace3844
replies.text: @normalchannel8493  That's an excellent question. We don't know the nature of the functionalization Robert performed, so it's tough to say. But one thing which has been done is to grow a polymer "comb" on spots on the graphene, which bonds to the polymer matrix through Van der Waals forces. If the fraction of graphene is low and it is well dispersed, it will be pretty much surrounded by the polymer matrix and all the combs will have something to bond to. I suppose as the fraction of graphene is increased, more and more you will have graphene platelets next to graphene platelets and they will just slide over each other. That is the long answer. The short answer is I don't know.

author: @handleismyhandle
text:
RMS and others, a new idea is a take on the Tesla turbine, combined with triboelectricity. . . Closely spaced ribbons (at least one conductive, spaced and tightned probably according to boundary layer thickness separation) of opposite triboelectric series position vibrating together harmonically like a harp in the wind, making and breaking contact along their length many times per second. If properly insulated (not grounded or near grounded objects, I would use a pair of hydrogen balloons to elevate the structure), it should convert a high percentage of incoming wind to high voltage electricity that can be stepped down to lower voltage AC using a Tesla coil. 

Another idea is a long flexible but rigid rod containing piezoelectric material connected to a buoy that will vibrate in a resonant fashion with the rise and fall of each incoming wave. 

Hope somebody else will build these as well, I don't want to keep the ideas to myself. Thanks.

author: @BenCollinsDigital
text: Curious about that recipe.... I understand it's proprietary.  Oddly, I don't see many experiments like this.  I wanted to mention that one issue with armor is the rigidity. I was thinking that small plates like scale mail or rings like chain mail might make the most sense, but I wanted to point out other flexible structures like Honeycomb: http://cnet4.cbsistatic.com/hub/i/r/2008/11/16/f178fcc8-fdc3-11e2-8c7c-d4ae52e62bcc/resize/570xauto/b64dbf290d314219bc0b71d16532a7da/Humvee_tire.jpg -- While I very much appreciate research into bullet proofing, there is a desperate need for IED protection as well. Plate shielding on the bottom of vehicles is one thing, but in terms of design I think we need to think a little more outside the box in terms of rigidity.

replies.author: @Michael-bc3es
replies.text: Ben Collins maybe a solution of nanosillica with propylene glycol. When subjected to force it goes from liquid to solid and soaks up a lot if force. They've used it to made Kevlar vests life proof.

author: @Broseficus
text:
That is some really dense and conducive material. I wonder if the bonding that happens is due to the graphene compound filling in all of the nano scale cracks, grooves, and otherwise invisible indentations to the naked eye of the host material. Good to hear that your team has the material almost to where you want it as a finished product. Hopefully the good Dr. Smith will do some videos tests on heat, tensile, breaking point, load bearing etc. Nothing too educational, after all some information should be kept proprietary. But some live demos would be really fun to watch! Also, I like how you are allowing other member of your team to showcase your developments. Maybe have them introduce themselves at the start of the video so that newcomers to the channel don't confuse them for the good Dr.?

Keep up the great work!

author: @gabbibrown9048
text: can it be combined with metals? such as copper to further increase its electrical capabilities? I.e. can liquid copper be combined in the early phases of making the plastic to give you a new kind plastic metal hybrid?

replies.author: @fwgsteve3806
replies.text: +Gabbi Brown Unfortunately we cannot answer that, but only because we haven't tried it. But it may be well worth a look at in the future.

replies.author: @Blendedasian
replies.text:
Gabbi Brown copper melts way hotter than most plastics, so trying to mix it in would most probably burn the plastic away.
But conductivity could be improved by adding very fine powder to the whole block while still liquid, electroplating from an ionic solution with a DC current or even molding the plastic around a solid metal core with external contact points and routing the electrical connections to such contacts.
